company.name: Zoetis
company.description: Zoetis develops and sells medicines, vaccines, diagnostics, and related services for companion animals and livestock.

overview.title: Business mix
paragraphs: Zoetis is a global animal-health company whose portfolio spans companion-animal and livestock medicines, vaccines, diagnostics, biopharmaceuticals, genetic tests, and digital tools.
The 2025 Form 10-K reported $9.47 billion of revenue, with companion-animal products generating $6.59 billion and product-category strength concentrated in parasiticides, vaccines, and dermatology.

overview.title: Registry fit
paragraphs: Zoetis is a strong incumbent case because its leading products depend on prescription channels, veterinarian trust, regulatory approvals, clinical evidence, manufacturing quality, brand recognition, and large-scale distribution.

Free, open, or decentralized pressure is more credible around discovery tooling, comparative outcomes data, parasite surveillance, cooperative procurement, and post-exclusivity access than as a direct unsupervised replacement for approved veterinary drugs.

moatNarrative: Zoetis has a high moat because animal medicines and vaccines are regulated products sold through trusted veterinary and producer channels. Its recurring companion-animal franchises, especially parasiticides and dermatology, benefit from clinical familiarity, pet-owner demand, prescription workflows, safety monitoring, and global commercial reach.

The moat is not absolute. The 2025 Form 10-K notes channel pressure from internet retailers and big-box outlets, and pet owners may substitute lower-cost human health products in some cases. Still, replacing Zoetis's approved products requires evidence, pharmacovigilance, manufacturing control, and veterinary adoption rather than just lower-cost chemistry.
decentralizationNarrative: Zoetis's finished medicines are difficult to decentralize safely because dosing, contraindications, adverse-event reporting, supply quality, veterinary supervision, and regulatory approvals are essential parts of the product.

The decentralizable layers sit around the product: open drug-discovery infrastructure, clinic-owned outcome registries, federated parasite-risk data, transparent comparative-effectiveness studies, and cooperative purchasing or generic-access programs once exclusivity and regulatory constraints allow substitution.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 251-275; rankApprox uses the cohort midpoint because the manifest did not include an exact rank.

moat.rationale: Prescription veterinary medicines, approved parasiticides, dermatology franchises, global distribution, veterinarian trust, regulatory compliance, and recurring companion-animal demand create strong switching and trust barriers.

decentralizability.rationale: Approved veterinary drugs are hard to decentralize directly, but discovery tooling, outcome evidence, parasite surveillance, and clinic-owned coordination layers can be opened or federated.

profitability.rationale: The 2025 Form 10-K reported $2.67 billion of GAAP net income attributable to Zoetis on $9.47 billion of revenue, implying a strong profit margin for an animal-health pharmaceutical platform.

peRatio.rationale: StockAnalysis reported a trailing P/E ratio of 12.46 for ZTS after the June 26, 2026 close.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported Zoetis's market capitalization at about $31.89 billion as of June 2026, with a June 26, 2026 end-of-day figure of $31.89 billion.

products.name: Apoquel
products.summary: Apoquel is Zoetis's oclacitinib-based prescription product family for allergic itch and atopic dermatitis in dogs at least 12 months old.
products.whyItMatters: Apoquel anchors Zoetis's dermatology franchise by combining a high-volume canine itch indication, veterinarian prescribing, branded safety information, and repeat pet-owner demand.
replacementSketch: A credible open replacement is not an unsupervised copy of Apoquel. It would need validated chemistry, dosing, adverse-event monitoring, veterinary oversight, manufacturing quality, and regulatory approval.

Near-term open pressure is more plausible through shared dermatology outcome data, open target discovery, computational lead optimization, and cooperative funding for post-exclusivity comparative studies or lower-cost approved therapies.

alternatives.name: Open Targets Platform
alternatives.summary: Open Targets is a freely available open-source platform that aggregates public data to help identify and prioritize therapeutic drug targets.

openness.rationale: The project describes its platform as freely available and open source, with public data aggregation for target prioritization.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It decentralizes access to early target-selection intelligence, but it does not replace regulated veterinary development, manufacturing, or prescribing.

readiness.rationale: The platform is usable for research workflows today, though it is not a finished veterinary dermatology therapy.

costLeverage.rationale: Open target evidence can reduce duplicated early research effort, while downstream animal studies, approval, and manufacturing remain expensive.

alternatives.name: Open Free Energy
alternatives.summary: Open Free Energy is an MIT-licensed open-source ecosystem for binding free-energy calculations used in pharmaceutical lead optimization.

openness.rationale: The project states that its software is developed publicly on GitHub and released under the permissive MIT license.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It distributes computational lead-optimization tooling but does not decentralize clinical validation, veterinary prescribing, or regulated production.

readiness.rationale: The toolkit is practical for specialist computational chemistry teams, but it is not a direct therapeutic product.

costLeverage.rationale: Open molecular simulation tools can lower dependence on proprietary modeling stacks during discovery, though animal-health development costs remain substantial.

disruptionConcepts.name: Clinic-owned dermatology outcomes commons
disruptionConcepts.summary: Independent veterinary clinics could pool de-identified canine dermatology outcomes, adverse events, dosing patterns, and follow-up data into a federated commons that supports comparative treatment evidence and open discovery programs without one branded drug maker owning the evidence layer.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market structure changes when clinics and researchers collectively own comparative evidence and can fund open or post-exclusivity alternatives, weakening the informational advantage around branded dermatology franchises even if approved drugs remain regulated.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federation and cooperative governance rather than Bitcoin. Clinics retain local records while publishing signed, standardized outcome summaries into shared registries.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Clinics, dermatologists, shelters, academic researchers, and open-tool maintainers coordinate around shared data schemas, outcome measures, adverse-event fields, and study protocols; cooperative members fund priority analyses and validation studies.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Veterinarian-attested records, prescription identifiers, audit logs, de-identification checks, duplicate detection, and independent statistical review constrain fabricated outcomes or sponsor-biased reporting.
failureModes: Data quality and case-mix bias could make the commons less reliable than controlled clinical trials.
Privacy, liability, and commercial sensitivity may limit clinic participation or prevent sufficiently broad coverage.
Open evidence does not remove the need for approved products, pharmacovigilance, and manufacturing quality.
adoptionPath: Start with voluntary outcome tracking for allergic itch visits and medication switches across open or exportable clinic systems.
Use the registry to prioritize comparative studies, adverse-event monitoring, and open discovery work for lower-cost approved therapies or post-exclusivity options.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept decentralizes the evidence and study-prioritization layer while leaving regulated prescribing and manufacturing intact.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Federated data and open therapeutic science tools exist, but veterinary clinics would need governance, incentives, and standardized outcomes.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Data pooling and analytics are feasible; producing an approved lower-cost dermatology medicine remains much harder.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The commons could pressure evidence control and post-exclusivity economics, but Apoquel's prescription status, safety profile, and brand trust remain powerful near-term advantages.

products.name: Simparica Trio
products.summary: Simparica Trio is a monthly prescription chewable for dogs that combines protection against heartworm disease, fleas, multiple tick species, roundworms, hookworms, and flea tapeworm risk via flea control.
products.whyItMatters: Simparica Trio is a major Zoetis parasiticide franchise because it bundles broad parasite protection into a repeat monthly veterinary prescription and pet-owner habit.
replacementSketch: A direct open substitute would still need approved active ingredients, species-specific dosing, safety data, quality manufacturing, and veterinary supervision.

The more credible replacement path is an open parasite-risk and treatment-evidence layer that helps clinics choose targeted preventatives, detect resistance or adverse-event patterns, and coordinate lower-cost approved options when clinically appropriate.

alternatives.name: Therapeutics Data Commons
alternatives.summary: Therapeutics Data Commons is an open-science and open-source resource for AI-ready therapeutic datasets, benchmarks, data functions, and molecule generation workflows.

openness.rationale: TDC describes itself as an open-science initiative and provides an open GitHub repository for its Python library and resources.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It broadens access to discovery datasets and benchmarks, though it does not replace regulated parasiticide approval or manufacturing.

readiness.rationale: The library and datasets are usable for machine-learning research today, but veterinary antiparasitic development requires separate validation.

costLeverage.rationale: Shared benchmarks and datasets can reduce duplicated discovery work, but safety testing, field studies, and registration remain large cost centers.

alternatives.name: Open Free Energy
alternatives.summary: Open Free Energy provides open-source molecular simulation tools that can support lead optimization for small-molecule drug discovery programs.

openness.rationale: The software is developed publicly on GitHub and released under the MIT license.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It decentralizes specialist computational tooling but does not decentralize field efficacy studies, veterinary prescribing, or regulated production.

readiness.rationale: The toolkit is production-relevant for computational discovery teams, but not a standalone antiparasitic product.

costLeverage.rationale: Open binding free-energy tools can lower discovery-tooling costs and improve reproducibility, while downstream product development remains expensive.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated parasite-risk and prevention commons
disruptionConcepts.summary: Veterinary clinics, diagnostic labs, shelters, and researchers could run a federated parasite-risk network that pools location-aware test results, treatment outcomes, adverse events, and resistance signals to guide prevention choices and fund open antiparasitic discovery priorities.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market structure changes if parasite prevention advice and evidence are governed by a clinic-owned network rather than bundled primarily around branded monthly preventatives and manufacturer-controlled promotion.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The core role is decentralized coordination, not Bitcoin. Federated nodes let clinics and labs keep local control while contributing signed evidence to shared risk maps, treatment guidance, and open research bounties.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Clinics and labs submit standardized parasite tests, prescriptions, geography, outcomes, and adverse-event observations; researchers and tool maintainers publish models and candidate discovery tasks; cooperative members vote on validation studies and funding priorities.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Vet- or lab-attested records, signed submissions, geospatial anomaly checks, audit trails, external proficiency testing, and public model-version histories constrain fake prevalence reports, biased manufacturer uploads, and spoofed treatment outcomes.
failureModes: Sparse or biased clinic participation could produce misleading regional risk estimates.
A parasite-risk commons may improve targeting but still cannot replace broad-spectrum prescription products where veterinarians judge them necessary.
Open discovery outputs may be enclosed by later sponsors unless funding and licensing terms preserve access commitments.
adoptionPath: Begin with de-identified parasite test and prevention-outcome feeds from clinics, shelters, and labs in high-participation regions.
Use the resulting evidence to support targeted prevention guidance, cooperative purchasing of approved options, and open discovery or repurposing studies for future antiparasitic candidates.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Federated clinic and lab data directly decentralize parasite-risk intelligence and treatment-evidence governance.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The data and modeling primitives exist, but broad participation from clinics, labs, shelters, and researchers would require strong governance and incentives.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Evidence pooling and modeling are feasible before drug development; replacing or materially reducing reliance on broad-spectrum branded preventatives is much harder.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept pressures manufacturer-controlled risk messaging and could support lower-cost approved choices, but Simparica Trio's broad label, monthly habit, and prescription channel remain strong.

summaryMarkdown: Zoetis refresh drafted as a high-moat, low-direct-decentralizability animal-health pharmaceutical incumbent. The strongest open pressure is modeled around clinic-owned evidence, parasite-risk federation, and open therapeutic discovery tooling rather than direct unsupervised substitutes for approved veterinary drugs.
